quizQuestion: Pteranodon was a flying reptile — what group does it belong to?
explanation: Pteranodon was a pterosaur, not a dinosaur. Pterosaurs were a distinct lineage of flying reptiles that lived alongside dinosaurs but are not classified within Dinosauria. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
visualDescription: A large winged reptile soaring over Cretaceous seas, crested head sweeping back, leathery wings spread wide against a pale sky

quizQuestion: Pteranodon had a wingspan of approximately how many meters?
explanation: Pteranodon had a wingspan of about 6 to 7 meters, making it one of the largest flying animals of the Cretaceous. It soared over the Western Interior Seaway using thermals like modern sea birds. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
visualDescription: Top-down silhouette of a Pteranodon with outstretched wings, length measurement lines showing its impressive 6.5-meter span

quizQuestion: Which Cretaceous pterosaur had a distinctive backward-pointing head crest and was completely toothless?
explanation: Pteranodon's name literally means 'winged and toothless'. It had a prominent backward-pointing crest and caught fish over the Western Interior Seaway that covered much of North America. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
visualDescription: A crested pterosaur skimming ocean waves, beak dipping toward the water to snatch fish, elongated crest streaming behind its head

quizQuestion: Which pterosaur holds the record as the largest flying animal ever known, with a wingspan of about 10.5 meters?
explanation: Quetzalcoatlus is the largest known flying animal in Earth's history, with a wingspan of roughly 10 to 11 meters. When standing on the ground it was giraffe-sized — about 5 meters tall. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
visualDescription: An enormous pterosaur standing upright among trees, its head level with the canopy, tiny humans shown beside it for scale

quizQuestion: The giant pterosaur Quetzalcoatlus was named after which Aztec deity?
explanation: Quetzalcoatlus takes its name from Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god of Aztec mythology. The pterosaur was discovered in Texas (Hell Creek Formation) and named in 1975 by Douglas Lawson. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
visualDescription: An ancient Aztec stone carving of the feathered serpent deity beside a skeletal reconstruction of the giant pterosaur named after it

quizQuestion: When standing on the ground, Quetzalcoatlus was roughly the same height as which modern animal?
explanation: Quetzalcoatlus stood approximately 5 meters tall when on the ground — roughly the height of a giraffe. Despite being a flier, it likely stalked prey on land, similar to a modern stork. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
visualDescription: A Quetzalcoatlus walking on land beside a giraffe, both reaching the same height, dwarfing humans nearby

quizQuestion: Which Early Jurassic pterosaur is named for having two distinct types of teeth — unusual among pterosaurs?
explanation: Dimorphodon's name means 'two-form tooth', reflecting its unique dentition with two different tooth shapes. It was found on the Jurassic Coast of Dorset by Mary Anning and described by Richard Owen in 1859. Source: Wikipedia/Dimorphodon
visualDescription: A small pterosaur with a disproportionately large head and short wings clambering along coastal cliffs, its mixed dentition visible in the open beak

quizQuestion: The Dimorphodon pterosaur fossil was discovered on the Jurassic Coast of Dorset by which pioneering fossil hunter?
explanation: Mary Anning found the Dimorphodon fossil in 1828 on the Jurassic Coast near Lyme Regis, Dorset. It was the first pterosaur specimen found outside Germany. Anning also discovered the first complete Ichthyosaurus and the first Plesiosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Dimorphodon
visualDescription: A Victorian-era woman fossil hunter on rugged coastal cliffs, examining a winged reptile fossil embedded in limestone

quizQuestion: Mosasaurus was a giant marine reptile more closely related to which group of modern animals than to dinosaurs?
explanation: Mosasaurus was not a dinosaur or plesiosaur — it was a mosasaurid, part of a lineage most closely related to modern monitor lizards (Varanidae). It had a double-hinged jaw similar to snakes for swallowing large prey. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
visualDescription: A massive serpentine sea creature with four paddle-like limbs surging through dark ocean water, its lizard-like head agape with rows of conical teeth

quizQuestion: The Mosasaurus was first discovered near which city in the Netherlands — a name reflected in its meaning 'Meuse lizard'?
explanation: The first Mosasaurus specimens were found near Maastricht in the Netherlands, near the Meuse River (Mosa in Latin), giving the animal its name. It was one of the most fearsome apex predators of the Late Cretaceous seas. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
visualDescription: A fossil skull of a giant marine reptile displayed in a museum case, a map inset showing Maastricht near the Meuse River in the Netherlands

quizQuestion: Which giant marine reptile was the apex ocean predator of the Late Cretaceous, famously featured in Jurassic World?
explanation: Mosasaurus was the dominant marine predator of the Late Cretaceous oceans. Up to 13 meters long and 14 tonnes, it could consume virtually any prey. It featured prominently in the Jurassic World film franchise. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
visualDescription: An enormous marine predator breaching the ocean surface to seize prey in its massive jaws, Late Cretaceous seas around it

quizQuestion: Who found the first complete Ichthyosaurus skeleton near Lyme Regis, England, around 1811 at approximately age 12?
explanation: Mary Anning discovered the first complete Ichthyosaurus skeleton near Lyme Regis around 1811 when she was about 12 years old. This landmark find launched her career as one of history's greatest fossil hunters. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
visualDescription: A young Victorian girl and her brother on a rocky beach, uncovering an extraordinary dolphin-shaped fossil skeleton from the cliffs of Lyme Regis

quizQuestion: Which dolphin-shaped marine reptile evolved a streamlined body through convergent evolution — looking like a fish but being a reptile?
explanation: Ichthyosaurus evolved a dolphin-like body shape independently of dolphins — a classic example of convergent evolution. Unlike fish, it breathed air and gave birth to live young. Fossils with embryos inside have been discovered. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
visualDescription: A sleek dolphin-shaped marine reptile cutting through Jurassic seas, its large eye adapted for deep diving, small embryo visible in a comparative diagram

quizQuestion: Unlike many reptiles, Ichthyosaurus gave birth how — as proven by fossils showing mothers with embryos?
explanation: Exceptional fossils of Ichthyosaurus have been found with embryos preserved inside the mother's body, proving they gave birth to live young rather than laying eggs — an adaptation to their fully aquatic lifestyle. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
visualDescription: A museum-quality fossil slab showing an Ichthyosaurus skeleton with several small embryo skeletons preserved inside the rib cage

quizQuestion: Which long-necked marine reptile with four paddle-like flippers is often cited as the inspiration for the Loch Ness Monster myth?
explanation: Plesiosaurus, with its long neck, small head, and four flippers, inspired the popular image of the Loch Ness Monster. In reality, plesiosaurs were ocean-dwellers and could not raise their long necks far above the water surface. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
visualDescription: A graceful long-necked marine reptile gliding underwater, four large paddles propelling it, its small head emerging just above the surface in misty waters

quizQuestion: In what year did Mary Anning discover the first complete Plesiosaurus fossil at Lyme Regis?
explanation: Mary Anning found the first complete Plesiosaurus at Lyme Regis in 1823. The specimen was described by William Conybeare and was so unusual that the Geological Society initially questioned its authenticity. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
visualDescription: A beautifully preserved fossil skeleton of a long-necked plesiosaur laid out on a rock surface, the distinctive paddle-like limbs and long neck clearly visible

quizQuestion: True or false: Plesiosaurus could raise its long neck high above the water surface like a swan.
explanation: False — despite popular depictions, plesiosaurs could not raise their necks high above water. The vertebral structure prevented swan-like poses. The popular image of the Loch Ness Monster with its neck raised is anatomically impossible for a plesiosaur. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
visualDescription: A side-by-side comparison: a popular (incorrect) depiction of a plesiosaur with neck raised high vs. a scientifically accurate view of the neck held mostly horizontal near the water

quizQuestion: Which 150-million-year-old fossil is the most famous transitional link between non-avian dinosaurs and modern birds?
explanation: Archaeopteryx, found in the Solnhofen limestone of Bavaria in 1861, had feathered wings like a bird but also retained dinosaurian features: teeth, clawed fingers on its wings, and a long bony tail. It remains the most celebrated transitional fossil in paleontology. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
visualDescription: A beautifully preserved fossil in pale limestone showing the outline of feathered wings, teeth, and a long bony tail — the perfect blend of bird and dinosaur

quizQuestion: In what year was the first Archaeopteryx specimen found — just two years after Darwin published On the Origin of Species?
explanation: The first Archaeopteryx specimen was discovered in 1861, only two years after Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859). Its intermediate features between dinosaurs and birds provided powerful support for Darwin's theory at exactly the right moment. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
visualDescription: A timeline comparing Darwin's book publication in 1859 with the Archaeopteryx discovery in 1861, a fossil slab shown alongside Darwin's famous portrait

quizQuestion: Although Archaeopteryx had feathered wings like a bird, it still possessed which distinctly un-bird-like feature?
explanation: Archaeopteryx retained teeth in its beak, along with clawed fingers on its wings and a long bony tail — all dinosaurian features. Modern birds have none of these, making Archaeopteryx a crucial transitional specimen. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
visualDescription: A close-up reconstruction of an Archaeopteryx head showing its feathered body combined with a toothed jaw, unlike any modern bird

quizQuestion: Which small omnivorous dinosaur from Argentina, dating to 231 million years ago, is one of the earliest known dinosaurs — its name meaning 'dawn thief'?
explanation: Eoraptor, meaning 'dawn thief', lived approximately 231 million years ago during the Late Triassic. It was discovered in the Ischigualasto Formation of Argentina by Paul Sereno in 1991 and provides key insight into the earliest dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
visualDescription: A small, lightly built bipedal dinosaur darting through a Triassic landscape of sparse ferns and conifer trees, tiny and fast compared to later giants

quizQuestion: Eoraptor was discovered in 1991 in Argentina's Ischigualasto Formation by which American paleontologist?
explanation: Paul Sereno and his team discovered Eoraptor in 1991 in the Ischigualasto Formation, also known as the Valle de la Luna, in the San Juan Province of Argentina. Sereno is known for discovering some of the oldest and largest dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
visualDescription: A paleontologist carefully brushing rock away from a small dinosaur skeleton in an arid Argentine desert landscape

quizQuestion: Eoraptor, one of the earliest known dinosaurs, lived during which geological period approximately 231 million years ago?
explanation: Eoraptor lived during the Late Triassic period, approximately 231 million years ago. Dinosaurs first appeared during the Triassic, shortly after the catastrophic Permian-Triassic mass extinction wiped out most of Earth's species. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
visualDescription: A geological timeline showing the Triassic period highlighted, with a small Eoraptor silhouette placed at 231 million years ago

quizQuestion: The Triassic period lasted from approximately how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
explanation: The Triassic period spanned from approximately 252 to 201 million years ago. It began after the devastating Permian-Triassic mass extinction (the 'Great Dying') and ended with another extinction event that opened the door for dinosaurs to dominate. Source: Wikipedia/Triassic
visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Triassic band highlighted in warm red-orange, spanning from 252 to 201 million years ago

quizQuestion: During the Triassic period, the first dinosaurs appeared approximately how many million years ago?
explanation: The earliest known dinosaurs, including Eoraptor and Herrerasaurus, appeared approximately 231 million years ago in the Late Triassic. They arose in what is now Argentina, shortly after the Permian-Triassic mass extinction had cleared ecological niches. Source: Wikipedia/Triassic
visualDescription: A Late Triassic landscape with small early dinosaurs running among larger non-dinosaur reptiles, the southern supercontinent Gondwana stretching to the horizon

quizQuestion: During the Triassic period, all continents were joined in which single supercontinent?
explanation: During the Triassic, Earth's landmasses were merged into the supercontinent Pangaea. The hot, dry climate created by this vast landmass shaped the environments where the first dinosaurs evolved. Pangaea began breaking apart in the Early Jurassic (~175 mya). Source: Wikipedia/Pangaea
visualDescription: A globe showing all continents fused together into the supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by the vast Panthalassa ocean, during the Triassic period

quizQuestion: The Jurassic period spanned approximately from how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
explanation: The Jurassic period lasted from approximately 201 to 145 million years ago. It saw the dominance of sauropods as the largest land animals, the evolution of the first birds from theropod dinosaurs, and the break-up of Pangaea. Source: Wikipedia/Jurassic
visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Jurassic band highlighted in green, spanning from 201 to 145 million years ago

quizQuestion: During the Late Jurassic (~150 mya), which famous transitional fossil marks the evolution of birds from theropod dinosaurs?
explanation: Archaeopteryx, dating to approximately 150 million years ago in the Late Jurassic, is the most famous evidence that birds evolved from theropod dinosaurs. Its discovery in 1861 provided crucial support for Darwin's theory of evolution published just two years earlier. Source: Wikipedia/Jurassic
visualDescription: A fossil slab of Archaeopteryx in limestone beside an illustration of the living animal — a feathered creature mid-way between dinosaur and bird

quizQuestion: The Cretaceous period lasted from approximately how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
explanation: The Cretaceous period spanned from approximately 145 to 66 million years ago — the longest period of the Mesozoic Era. It ended with the K-Pg mass extinction event caused by the Chicxulub asteroid impact. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous
visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Cretaceous band highlighted in teal-blue, spanning from 145 to 66 million years ago with a dramatic impact marker at the end

quizQuestion: The asteroid that caused the K-Pg mass extinction 66 million years ago was estimated to be approximately how many kilometers in diameter?
explanation: The Chicxulub asteroid that struck Earth 66 million years ago is estimated to have been 10 to 15 kilometers in diameter. The impact released energy equivalent to billions of nuclear bombs, triggering wildfires, a dust cloud blocking sunlight, and a global 'impact winter'. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
visualDescription: A massive glowing asteroid trailing fire plunging toward a Cretaceous Earth, dinosaurs visible on the ground below as the sky ignites

quizQuestion: The Chicxulub crater, formed by the asteroid that wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs, is located beneath which region?
explanation: The Chicxulub impact crater lies beneath the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico and the Gulf of Mexico. About 180 kilometers wide, it is one of the largest confirmed impact structures on Earth. The impact 66 million years ago caused the K-Pg mass extinction. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
visualDescription: A satellite map showing the circular outline of the Chicxulub crater partially offshore the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico, with an impact illustration overlay

quizQuestion: The K-Pg extinction event 66 million years ago wiped out approximately what percentage of all species on Earth?
explanation: The Cretaceous-Paleogene (K-Pg) extinction event eliminated approximately 75% of all species on Earth. Non-avian dinosaurs were entirely wiped out, while birds (avian dinosaurs), mammals, crocodilians, and some other groups survived. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
visualDescription: A dramatic infographic showing 75% of animal silhouettes grayed out and crossed, with surviving groups — birds, mammals, crocodiles — highlighted in color

quizQuestion: Which British scientist coined the term 'Dinosauria' in 1842, meaning 'terrible lizards'?
explanation: Richard Owen coined the word 'Dinosauria' in 1842 to group together Megalosaurus, Iguanodon, and Hylaeosaurus as a distinct class of ancient reptiles. He also founded the Natural History Museum in London. Source: Wikipedia/Richard Owen
visualDescription: A Victorian-era portrait of a distinguished scientist beside a reconstruction of a large dinosaur skeleton, the word 'Dinosauria' written in ornate 19th-century script

quizQuestion: Richard Owen, who coined the term 'Dinosauria', founded which famous museum in London?
explanation: Richard Owen was instrumental in founding the Natural History Museum in London, which opened in 1881. He believed natural history collections should be accessible to the public, not just scientists. Source: Wikipedia/Richard Owen
visualDescription: The iconic Romanesque facade of London's Natural History Museum, with a giant dinosaur skeleton displayed prominently in the entrance hall

quizQuestion: Which pioneering 19th-century fossil hunter from Lyme Regis discovered the first Ichthyosaurus, first Plesiosaurus, and first pterosaur outside Germany?
explanation: Mary Anning (1799–1847) made world-changing fossil discoveries along the Jurassic Coast of Dorset: the first complete Ichthyosaurus (~1811), the first Plesiosaurus (1823), and the Dimorphodon pterosaur (1828). She was largely uncredited in her lifetime due to gender and social class. Source: Wikipedia/Mary Anning
visualDescription: A determined young woman in Victorian dress walking the fossil-strewn cliffs of Lyme Regis with a hammer, loyal dog by her side, a stormy grey sea behind her

quizQuestion: Which pioneering fossil hunter is popularly believed to have inspired the tongue twister 'She sells sea shells by the sea shore'?
explanation: Mary Anning sold fossils to support her family on the beaches of Lyme Regis, and many believe she inspired the famous tongue twister 'She sells sea shells by the sea shore'. Although the connection is disputed, it reflects how well-known she was in her era. Source: Wikipedia/Mary Anning
visualDescription: A Victorian seaside scene with a woman selling fossils from a basket on a pebble beach, tourists examining ammonites, belemnites, and ichthyosaur bones

quizQuestion: The 'Bone Wars' (1877–1892) were a fierce rivalry between Edward Drinker Cope and which other American paleontologist?
explanation: The Bone Wars pitted Edward Drinker Cope against Othniel Charles Marsh in a bitter race to discover and name the most dinosaur species. Together they described 142 new species using methods that included espionage, bribery, and sabotage. Source: Wikipedia/Bone Wars
visualDescription: Two mustachioed Victorian-era scientists glaring at each other across a map of the American West dotted with fossil excavation sites, shovel in each man's hand

quizQuestion: Marsh and Cope together discovered how many new dinosaur species during the Bone Wars?
explanation: Between them, Othniel Charles Marsh and Edward Drinker Cope described approximately 142 new dinosaur species during the Bone Wars (1877–1892). Marsh named Apatosaurus, Brontosaurus, Stegosaurus, and Triceratops; Cope named Camarasaurus, Coelophysis, and many others. Source: Wikipedia/Bone Wars
visualDescription: A ledger-style infographic listing the dinosaur names discovered by Marsh and Cope, with a tally counter showing 142 species total

quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was the first to be formally named in science, described by William Buckland in 1824?
explanation: Megalosaurus, meaning 'great lizard', was formally named in 1824 by William Buckland — making it the first dinosaur to receive a scientific name. Iguanodon followed in 1825. Neither species had been identified as a 'dinosaur' yet, as Richard Owen didn't coin that term until 1842. Source: Wikipedia/Megalosaurus
visualDescription: A 19th-century engraving of Megalosaurus as scientists imagined it — a hulking quadrupedal beast — beside the modern bipedal reconstruction

quizQuestion: Pangaea, the supercontinent that existed during the Triassic, began breaking apart during which geological period approximately 175 million years ago?
explanation: Pangaea began breaking apart in the Early Jurassic, approximately 175 million years ago. The separation created the proto-Atlantic Ocean and split the landmass into Laurasia (north) and Gondwana (south), eventually leading to the modern continents. Source: Wikipedia/Pangaea
visualDescription: A sequence of four globe maps showing Pangaea intact in the Triassic, beginning to rift in the Jurassic, splitting into Laurasia and Gondwana, and approaching modern continental positions

quizQuestion: Where were the feathered dinosaur fossils discovered in the 1990s that revolutionized our understanding of dinosaur evolution?
explanation: Extraordinary feathered dinosaur fossils were discovered in Liaoning Province, China, starting in the 1990s. These included Sinosauropteryx (confirmed 1996) and many others, proving that feathers were widespread among theropod dinosaurs long before the evolution of birds. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
visualDescription: A Chinese paleontologist examining a beautifully preserved fossil showing clear feather impressions on a small theropod dinosaur in fine-grained shale

quizQuestion: In what year was Sinosauropteryx confirmed as the first feathered non-avian dinosaur, revolutionizing paleontology?
explanation: Sinosauropteryx was confirmed in 1996 as the first feathered non-avian dinosaur. Found in Liaoning Province, China, its discovery was part of a wave of Chinese feathered fossil finds that proved feathers evolved in theropod dinosaurs before the origin of birds. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
visualDescription: A milestone timeline in paleontology history, with 1996 marked for the Sinosauropteryx confirmation beside an illustration of the small feathered dinosaur

quizQuestion: In what year was physical proof discovered that Velociraptor had feathers — in the form of quill knobs on its arm bones?
explanation: In 2007, scientists confirmed that Velociraptor had feathers, discovering quill knobs (feather anchor points) on the ulna bone of its forearm. This matched similar structures found in modern birds and confirmed that the famous predator was feathered, not scaly. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
visualDescription: A diagram of a Velociraptor forearm bone with quill knobs highlighted, compared to a modern eagle wing bone showing the same anchoring structures

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Albertosaurus, a tyrannosaurid from Late Cretaceous Alberta, Canada. Smaller than T. rex but with a similar build, Albertosaurus was a fast predator whose fossils include a remarkable bonebed of 26 individuals found by Barnum Brown in 1910.
visualDescription: Illustration of a medium-sized bipedal theropod with a long narrow skull and small arms, similar in shape to T. rex but more slender

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Allosaurus, the apex predator of the Late Jurassic period. Up to 8.5 meters long, it dominated the Morrison Formation of North America alongside sauropods like Diplodocus and Brachiosaurus.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large Jurassic theropod with strong three-clawed arms, a muscular frame, and a broad skull with prominent brow ridges

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Ankylosaurus, the heavily armored herbivore of the Late Cretaceous. Its entire back was covered in bony plates called osteoderms, and its massive tail club could shatter predator bones.
visualDescription: Illustration of a low, wide, four-legged dinosaur covered in bony armor plates across its back and sides, with a large bony club at the end of its tail

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Apatosaurus (also known as Brontosaurus — both names are now scientifically valid). This massive sauropod had a very long neck and whip-like tail and lived during the Late Jurassic in North America.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive long-necked sauropod dinosaur with a thick body, very long horizontal neck, and a tapering whip-like tail

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Archaeopteryx, the famous transitional fossil between non-avian dinosaurs and birds. It had feathered wings like a bird, but also retained teeth, clawed fingers, and a long bony tail like a dinosaur.
visualDescription: Illustration of a small feathered creature with toothed beak, clawed wing tips, and a long tail — unmistakably between a dinosaur and a bird

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Argentinosaurus, one of the largest land animals ever to live. Estimated at up to 35 meters long and 70 tonnes, this titanosaur sauropod from Patagonia, Argentina lived during the Late Cretaceous.
visualDescription: Illustration of a colossal sauropod dinosaur dwarfing everything around it, its immense column-like legs supporting a massive barrel-shaped body and very long neck

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Baryonyx, a fish-eating spinosaurid from Early Cretaceous England. It had a long crocodile-like skull and a massive curved thumb claw — its name means 'heavy claw'. It was discovered in Surrey, England in 1983.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large theropod with a notably elongated crocodile-like skull, wading at the edge of a river, one enormous hooked claw prominently visible on its hand

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Brachiosaurus, a Late Jurassic sauropod famous for having its front legs longer than its hind legs — giving it a giraffe-like upward-reaching posture. It could reach vegetation high in the tree canopy.
visualDescription: Illustration of a sauropod with noticeably longer front legs than hind legs, holding its neck in a giraffe-like upright posture, browsing high in the treetops

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Carcharodontosaurus, the 'shark-toothed lizard' from Late Cretaceous North Africa. One of the largest carnivorous dinosaurs ever, it coexisted with Spinosaurus in the ancient rivers and deltas of what is now Morocco and Egypt.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive bipedal carnivore with a large skull, serrated shark-like teeth, and powerful build, set in a North African Cretaceous environment

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Carnotaurus, the 'meat-eating bull' from Late Cretaceous Argentina. It is uniquely identified by the two thick bull-like horns above its eyes — the only large theropod known to have horns.
visualDescription: Illustration of a bipedal theropod dinosaur with two distinctive bull-like horns jutting above its eyes, very reduced vestigial arms, and a muscular compact body

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Ceratosaurus, a Late Jurassic predator from the Morrison Formation. Its most distinctive feature is the single horn on its nose — giving it the name 'horned lizard'. It also had a row of bony osteoderms running down its back.
visualDescription: Illustration of a medium-sized Jurassic theropod with a prominent single horn on its nose, a row of bony ridges along its back, and a relatively large head

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Coelophysis, one of the earliest known dinosaurs from the Late Triassic period. A slender, fast predator from Ghost Ranch, New Mexico, hundreds of its skeletons were found together in one of the most spectacular dinosaur bonebeds ever discovered.
visualDescription: Illustration of a very slender, lightly built early theropod with a long narrow head, long neck, and gracile limbs — distinctly early Triassic in character

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Compsognathus, one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs at roughly 65 centimeters long — about the size of a chicken. It lived in Late Jurassic Europe and was found in the same Solnhofen limestone as Archaeopteryx.
visualDescription: Illustration of a very small theropod dinosaur — roughly chicken-sized — with a long neck, small arms, and an agile build, running across coastal mudflats

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Corythosaurus, a Late Cretaceous hadrosaur whose name means 'helmet lizard'. Its tall, rounded head crest functioned as a resonating chamber for producing distinctive low-frequency calls for communication within the herd.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large duck-billed hadrosaur with a distinctive tall rounded helmet-shaped crest on top of its head

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Deinonychus, the 'terrible claw'. At 3.4 meters long, it was the real inspiration for the Jurassic Park 'Velociraptors' — which were actually based on Deinonychus, not the turkey-sized true Velociraptor.
visualDescription: Illustration of a feathered dromaeosaurid — about the size of a large turkey or wolf — with a distinctive raised sickle claw on each hind foot, in a dynamic stalking pose

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Dilophosaurus, the 'two-crested lizard' from Early Jurassic Arizona. It is identified by the distinctive pair of thin parallel crests running along its skull. Despite what Jurassic Park shows, it had no frill and no venom.
visualDescription: Illustration of a slender Early Jurassic theropod with two thin parallel bony crests running side-by-side along the top of its long skull

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Diplodocus, one of the longest dinosaurs at up to 26 meters. This Late Jurassic sauropod had a pencil-like neck, peg-like teeth for stripping leaves, and a whip-like tail. Casts of its skeleton were distributed to museums worldwide.
visualDescription: Illustration of a very long sauropod with a thin whip-like tail, relatively slender neck held nearly horizontally, and pencil-like teeth, walking across an open Jurassic floodplain

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Edmontosaurus, a large duck-billed hadrosaur from the very end of the Cretaceous. One of the last non-avian dinosaurs, it survived right up to the K-Pg extinction. Mummified specimens with preserved skin have been discovered.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large duck-billed hadrosaur without a pronounced head crest, bulky and quadrupedal, with a broad flat bill and thick skin impressions visible

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Eoraptor, one of the earliest known dinosaurs from 231 million years ago in Argentina. Only about 1.7 meters long, this small omnivore shows the primitive features that all dinosaurs descended from.
visualDescription: Illustration of a small, lightly built bipedal dinosaur in an open Triassic landscape, about the size of a large dog, with slender limbs and a small toothed head

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Gallimimus, an ornithomimid dinosaur whose name means 'chicken mimic'. One of the fastest dinosaurs, it had a toothless beak, long neck, and ostrich-like proportions. A herd of Gallimimus was famously featured in Jurassic Park.
visualDescription: Illustration of a long-legged, ostrich-like dinosaur with a toothless beak, long neck, and slender body built for speed, running across a Mongolian Cretaceous landscape

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Giganotosaurus, a massive predator from Patagonian Argentina that rivals T. rex in size at about 12.5 meters long. It hunted giant sauropods and had a long, slim skull with serrated blade-like teeth.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive South American theropod with an elongated skull and powerful build, set in a Cretaceous Patagonian landscape with giant sauropods in the background

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine reptile is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Ichthyosaurus, a dolphin-shaped marine reptile from the Early Jurassic. Despite looking like a dolphin, it is a reptile — an example of convergent evolution. Its fossils, including mothers with embryos, were famously discovered by Mary Anning.
visualDescription: Illustration of a streamlined dolphin-shaped marine reptile with large eyes, a vertical crescent tail fin, and dorsal fin, cutting through Jurassic seas

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Iguanodon, the second dinosaur ever formally named (1825). It is distinctive for its thumb spike — originally mistakenly placed on its nose. A large herbivore of the Early Cretaceous, 29 articulated skeletons were found in a Belgian mine in 1878.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large robust herbivorous dinosaur walking on two or four legs, with a distinctive spike thumb visible on its front hands

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Maiasaura, the 'good mother lizard'. Jack Horner's 1978 discovery of its nesting colonies in Montana proved dinosaurs cared for their young — overturning the image of dinosaurs as neglectful parents.
visualDescription: Illustration of a hadrosaur near a nest of eggs, with small hatchlings around it, the parent dinosaur attentively tending to its young

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine predator is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Mosasaurus, the dominant apex predator of the Late Cretaceous seas. Up to 13 meters long, this giant marine lizard (related to modern monitor lizards) had a double-hinged jaw and ruled the oceans until the K-Pg extinction.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive elongated marine predator with four paddle-like limbs, a long jaw full of conical teeth, and a powerful laterally flattened tail

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Oviraptor, a small feathered omnivore from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. Its name means 'egg thief' — it was wrongly accused of stealing Protoceratops eggs. Later discoveries proved it was actually brooding its own eggs, much like a bird.
visualDescription: Illustration of a small feathered omnivore with a toothless parrot-like beak, prominent head crest, and feathered arms, posed over a nest of eggs

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Pachycephalosaurus, the 'thick-headed lizard'. Its domed skull was up to 25 centimeters thick and may have been used in head-butting contests or flank displays to establish dominance.
visualDescription: Illustration of a bipedal herbivore with a massively thick rounded dome on top of its head, small bony knobs and spikes around the edges of the dome

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Parasaurolophus, instantly recognized by its long curved hollow crest sweeping back from the skull. CT scans confirmed the crest worked as a resonating chamber for producing trumpeting low-frequency calls.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large hadrosaur with a dramatic long curved crest sweeping backward from the top of its skull, highlighted against a Cretaceous forest background

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine reptile is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Plesiosaurus, a long-necked marine reptile of the Early Jurassic. With its four large paddle flippers and long neck, it inspired popular images of the Loch Ness Monster. It was first discovered by Mary Anning in 1823.
visualDescription: Illustration of a marine reptile with a very long thin neck, small head, barrel-shaped body, and four large paddle-like flippers, swimming gracefully through Jurassic seas

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Protoceratops, a small early ceratopsian from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. It lacked the large developed horns of its relatives, but had a distinctive neck frill. Famous for the 'fighting dinosaurs' fossil showing it locked in combat with a Velociraptor.
visualDescription: Illustration of a small four-legged herbivore with a prominent bony neck frill, a parrot-like beak, and no large facial horns, from Late Cretaceous Mongolia

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric flying reptile is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Pteranodon, a large Cretaceous pterosaur (flying reptile, not a dinosaur) with a distinctive backward-pointing head crest and a toothless beak. With a wingspan of about 6.5 meters, it soared over the ancient seas of North America.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large pterosaur with a prominent backward-pointing crest, long toothless beak, and leathery wings spread wide in flight over a coastal Cretaceous landscape

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric flying reptile is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Quetzalcoatlus (or a close relative), the largest flying animal ever known. With a wingspan of up to 10.5 meters, it was giraffe-sized when standing. Named after the Aztec feathered serpent god.
visualDescription: Illustration of a truly massive pterosaur with an enormous wingspan, long toothless beak, and giraffe-like proportions when standing on all four limbs on the ground

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Spinosaurus, the largest carnivorous dinosaur ever discovered at about 14 meters long. Identified by its spectacular sail of neural spines up to 1.65 meters tall, it was a semi-aquatic fish-eater with a crocodile-like snout.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive theropod with a dramatic sail of tall neural spines running along its back, a long crocodilian snout, and powerful arms, wading in a river

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Stegosaurus, the iconic Late Jurassic herbivore recognized by its double row of large bony plates along its back and four tail spikes (the 'thagomizer'). Despite its large body, it had a relatively small brain.
visualDescription: Illustration of a large four-legged herbivore with two staggered rows of tall diamond-shaped bony plates along its back and four pointed spikes at the end of its tail

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Styracosaurus, the 'spiked lizard' from Late Cretaceous Alberta. Distinguished by its spectacular frill adorned with multiple long spikes radiating outward, plus a single large nasal horn — more elaborate than its relative Triceratops.
visualDescription: Illustration of a ceratopsian dinosaur with a spectacular frill edged by multiple long radiating spikes and a prominent single nose horn — a more ornate version of Triceratops

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Therizinosaurus, the 'scythe lizard'. Despite being a theropod (the same group as T. rex), it was a herbivore with the longest claws of any known animal — up to one meter long. A bizarre and unique dinosaur from Late Cretaceous Mongolia.
visualDescription: Illustration of a bizarre theropod dinosaur with an enormous potbelly, feathered body, and impossibly long curved claws — up to a meter each — used for pulling down vegetation

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Triceratops, one of the most recognizable dinosaurs. With three facial horns and a large bony neck frill, it was a contemporary of T. rex in the Late Cretaceous. Evidence of combat between the two species has been found on fossilized bones.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive four-legged herbivore with three facial horns (two long brow horns and one shorter nose horn) and a large solid bony neck frill

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Tyrannosaurus rex, the iconic apex predator of the Late Cretaceous. Up to 12 meters long and 8.4 tonnes, it had banana-sized teeth, binocular vision, and tiny vestigial forelimbs. The Sue specimen at the Field Museum in Chicago is the most complete T. rex ever found.
visualDescription: Illustration of a massive bipedal predator with tiny arms and an enormous head filled with large teeth — the archetypal Tyrannosaurus rex pose

quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is Velociraptor, a feathered dromaeosaurid from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. Only turkey-sized in reality (not the large Jurassic Park version), it had a sickle-shaped killing claw on each foot and feathers confirmed by quill knobs found on its arm bones in 2007.
visualDescription: Illustration of a feathered dromaeosaurid roughly the size of a turkey, with a distinctive raised sickle claw on the second toe of each foot, running across a Mongolian Cretaceous landscape
